original_hypothesis.title: configUpdated Dependency Hot-Swap Enables Complete Fund Extraction via Malicious AccountManager
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The configUpdated() function, callable by configStorage, replaces all critical external contract references (accountManager, migrationManager, snuggeryManager, nftOverlord) in a single call without any timelock or validation. A compromised configStorage (or compromised admin who controls configStorage) can point accountManager to a malicious contract whose getPlayer() returns arbitrary data and whose forceHarvest() executes arbitrary logic. Since _lock calls accountManager.getPlayer and accountManager.forceHarvest BEFORE transferring tokens, and unlock calls forceHarvest before sending tokens, a malicious accountManager could manipulate state to enable unauthorized withdrawals or block legitimate ones.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The entire trust model of LockManager depends on the integrity of external contract references loaded from configStorage. There is no timelock, no multi-sig requirement on configUpdated, and no validation of the new addresses. If configStorage governance is compromised, the attacker gains full control over all user funds in the LockManager. This represents the highest-impact single point of failure in the contract's dependency chain.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: configUpdated at LockManager.sol lines 81-83 has onlyConfigStorage guard but no timelock; _reconfigure replaces all four dependency addresses atomically

proof_of_signal.pattern: _reconfigure() replaces accountManager, migrationManager, snuggeryManager, nftOverlord from configStorage without any validation or timelock

preconditions: ConfigStorage contract is compromised or admin key controlling configStorage is compromised
Attacker deploys malicious replacement contracts (e.g., fake AccountManager)
Attacker calls configStorage to update addresses and trigger configUpdated on LockManager

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No validation on replacement addresses in _reconfigure(); configStorage.getAddress returns attacker-controlled address
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious accountManager.getPlayer could allow any address to pass main-account checks; malicious forceHarvest could call back or manipulate state. Malicious migrationManager address would bypass NFT minting restrictions in _lock.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Total TVL of LockManager at risk; attacker can manipulate unlock flows or inject new lock entries

economic_questions: What governance model protects configStorage from unauthorized changes?
Is configStorage itself upgradeable or has its own admin key?
What is the total TVL held by LockManager?
validation_requirements: Map the full configStorage trust chain and access controls
Verify whether configStorage has timelock or multi-sig governance
Test deployment of LockManager with malicious configStorage to confirm full control over dependency references

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying malicious contracts and triggering configUpdated (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of all locked assets in the contract
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block once configStorage is compromised

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None; direct fund extraction from contract
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of user trust; protocol-ending event

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: configStorage access control model; who can call configStorage to trigger configUpdated
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify _reconfigure accepts any address from configStorage without validation

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
